name: Tuatha De Danann
description: The Tuatha Dé Danann, meaning "People of the Goddess Danu," are a supernatural race in Irish mythology. They represent the primary gods and goddesses of pre-Christian Ireland. Arriving in Ireland, according to legend, on dark clouds, they displaced the Fir Bolg and established their reign, ushering in an era of magic, artistry, and warfare. The Tuatha Dé Danann are depicted as possessing extraordinary skills in magic, craftsmanship, and warfare. They are associated with various aspects of nature, such as the sun, moon, earth, and sea, and are often portrayed as shapeshifters and wielders of potent magical artifacts. They eventually retreated into the Otherworld, becoming the fairy folk of Irish folklore, also known as the Aos Sí, after being defeated by the Milesians. Their legacy continues to resonate in Irish culture, symbolizing a powerful and enchanting past.
subtitle: The people of the goddess Danu, the gods and goddesses of pre-Christian Ireland.
appearance: The Tuatha Dé Danann are generally depicted as beautiful and radiant beings, often with fair skin, bright eyes, and long flowing hair. They are described as being tall and graceful, with an ethereal quality about them. Some are depicted with animal features, reflecting their connection to nature. Their clothing is often described as being richly adorned and colorful, reflecting their status as gods and goddesses.
habitat: Initially, they inhabited Ireland, ruling the land and establishing their culture. After their defeat by the Milesians, they retreated into the Otherworld, also known as Tír na nÓg, a realm of eternal youth and beauty. They are said to dwell in hidden glens, ancient mounds (síde), and beneath the waves.
behavior: The Tuatha Dé Danann are depicted as being wise, powerful, and often capricious. They are known for their skill in warfare and their ability to use magic to achieve their goals. While they are generally benevolent, they can also be vengeful and dangerous when crossed. They are deeply connected to the land and its people, and are often seen as protectors of Ireland.

keyMyths.title: The Battle of Mag Tuired
keyMyths.description: There are two significant Battles of Mag Tuired. The first saw the Tuatha Dé Danann defeat the Fir Bolg for control of Ireland. The second battle pitted them against the Fomorians, monstrous beings from the sea, resulting in a hard-fought victory for the Tuatha Dé Danann and the death of their king, Nuada.

keyMyths.title: The Dagda's Harp
keyMyths.description: The Dagda, a powerful and versatile god, possessed a magical harp that could control emotions and seasons. In one story, he uses his harp to lull the Fomorians to sleep, allowing the Tuatha Dé Danann to escape from captivity. The harp is a symbol of the Dagda's power and his connection to the natural world.

keyMyths.title: The Fate of Lugh
keyMyths.description: Lugh, a skilled warrior and craftsman, played a crucial role in the second Battle of Mag Tuired, ultimately slaying Balor of the Evil Eye. He is a figure of great importance in Irish mythology, representing skill, ingenuity, and the triumph of good over evil. He is a solar deity and master of all arts.

sources.author: Peter Berresford Ellis

sources.text: A comprehensive guide to the characters, creatures, and stories of Irish mythology.
